Full statement: there should be NO overlap of personnel between investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments; for a firewall to be effective in a multiservice firm, an employee should be on ONLY ONE side of the firewall at any time.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'There should be no overlap of personnel between the investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments. For a firewall to be effective in a multiservice firm, an employee should be on only one side of the firewall at any time.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The no-overlap principle is not seniority-based; spanning both sides defeats the barrier.

options.A: An analyst who assists investment bankers on a deal faces no restrictions once the project ends.
options.B: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company.
options.C: Research analysts may never provide any assistance to the investment banking department.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The analyst is treated as a banking-side participant — restricted until the information becomes public, not merely until the project ends.
rationales.B:
Full statement: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company; that analyst must then be treated as though he/she were an investment banker; must remain on investment banking side of the wall until any information he/she learns is publicly disclosed; in short, analyst cannot use any information learned in the course of the project for research purposes and cannot share that information with colleagues in research department.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'An analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to the investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when the firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company. That analyst must then be treated as though he or she were an investment banker; the analyst must remain on the investment banking side of the wall until any information he or she learns is publicly disclosed. In short, the analyst cannot use any information learned in the course of the project for research purposes and cannot share that information with colleagues in the research department.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. LIMITED assistance under carefully controlled, compliance-monitored circumstances is permitted.

options.A: The objective of a firewall is to eliminate all communication between departments permanently.
options.B: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments.
options.C: Reviews of cross-departmental communication may be performed by the requesting employees themselves.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The objective is a REPORTING SYSTEM in which authorized personnel review and approve necessary cross-wall communications, not a total ban.
rationales.B:
Full statement: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments; if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared; if sharing is necessary, compliance officer should coordinate the process of 'LOOKING OVER THE WALL' so that necessary information will be shared and integrity of procedure will be maintained.

Correct. Per Standard II(A) Guidance: 'A primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments. If an employee behind a firewall believes that he or she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side of the firewall, the employee should consult a designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing the information is necessary and how much information should be shared. If the sharing is necessary, the compliance officer should coordinate the process of "looking over the wall" so that the necessary information will be shared and the integrity of the procedure will be maintained.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Review and approval sit with authorized compliance personnel, not the interested employees.

options.A: There is no violation because commingled funds may contractually receive allocation priority over separate accounts.
options.B: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts.
options.C: Only the nondiscretionary accounts were treated unfairly; the discretionary tier was permissible.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Systematically giving one client group first access to hot issues does not treat all customers fairly under III(B).
rationales.B:
Full statement: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts; Jackson must execute orders on a systematic basis to be fair to all clients.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'The bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to the growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts. Jackson must execute orders on a systematic basis to be fair to all clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The tiered priority disadvantages every group behind the favored fund; the policy as a whole is the violation.

options.A: There is no violation because the written report distributed to all clients was comprehensive and accurate.
options.B: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B).
options.C: Roberts violated Standard II(A) by conveying material nonpublic information to his large clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Informally giving LARGE clients his higher earnings expectation denied other clients fair access to his opinion — a III(B) violation despite the accurate report.
rationales.B:
Full statement: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B); differential informal advice to only some clients constitutes selective disclosure that disadvantages other clients.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'By not sharing his opinion regarding the potential for a significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. His own earnings opinion is not issuer MNPI; the failure is unequal treatment of clients under fair dealing.

options.A: Hampton violated Standard III(B) because the allocation departed from a strictly pro-rata distribution.
options.B: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size.
options.C: Hampton violated Standard III(C) because a $5,000 position is unsuitable for clients who requested $10,000.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The required minimum lot size justified the departure; fair dealing does not demand strict pro-rata allocation when lot constraints apply.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size; with total allocation being significantly below amount requested, Hampton ensured that each client received at least minimum lot size of the issue and that filled allocations were close in [proportion].

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Hampton did not violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size. With the total allocation being significantly below the amount requested, Hampton ensured that each client received at least the minimum lot size of the issue and that the filled allocations were close in [proportion].'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Receiving a smaller-than-requested allocation raises no suitability issue.

options.A: There is no violation because clients with the same salary may properly receive the same recommendation.
options.B: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai.
options.C: The recommendation is unsuitable for both clients because zero-yield small-cap technology is inherently speculative.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Salary equality does not equalize circumstances; Lanai's low-volatility objective and education funding needs make the position unsuitable for her.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai; Smith violated Standard III(C) by applying Robertson's investment strategy to Lanai because the two clients' financial circumstances and objectives differ.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'In Robertson's case, the investment may be appropriate because of his financial circumstances and aggressive investment position, but this investment is not suitable for Lanai. Smith violated Standard III(C) by applying Robertson's investment strategy to Lanai because the two clients' financial circumstances and objectives differ.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. For Robertson — aggressive, high-net-worth — the investment may well be appropriate; suitability is client-specific.

options.A: Covered calls are unsuitable for risk-averse clients under any circumstances.
options.B: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis.
options.C: Suitability is judged option position by option position, without reference to the rest of the client's holdings.

rationales.A: Incorrect. With client education and portfolio-level analysis, income-enhancing covered calls can suit a risk-averse investor.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis; basic characteristics of entire portfolio will largely determine whether investment recommendations are taking client factors into account; here, McDowell properly considers investment in context of entire portfolio and thoroughly explains investment to client.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'When determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be the characteristics of the client's entire portfolio, not the characteristics of single securities on an issue-by-issue basis. The basic characteristics of the entire portfolio will largely determine whether investment recommendations are taking client factors into account. In this case, McDowell properly considers the investment in the context of the entire portfolio and thoroughly explains the investment to the client.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The example's lesson is the opposite: the ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not the single position, is the focus of suitability.

options.A: There is no violation because maximizing return is the trustee's overriding fiduciary duty.
options.B: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund.
options.C: There is no violation because ESG restrictions are aspirational guidance rather than binding constraints.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The updated IPS restriction is a binding constraint; expected return does not override it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund; in this case, a preference for incorporating ESG issues into investment process is clearly mandated; trustees have a duty to ensure fund's assets are invested in accordance with IPS.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider the constraints and unique circumstances of the beneficiaries of the pension fund. In this case, a preference for incorporating ESG issues into the investment process is clearly mandated. The trustees have a duty to ensure that the fund's assets are invested in accordance with the IPS. Any trustee who is required to abide by the Code and Standards, such as DeVries, would need to ensure that Van Cleef sells the inappropriate tobacco securities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Once written into the IPS, the health-products restriction binds the manager like any other constraint.

options.A: The member must execute any unsolicited client order immediately, since the account belongs to the client.
options.B: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client.
options.C: The member should permanently refuse unsuitable unsolicited orders and close the account if the client insists.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For a KNOWN-unsuitable request, the member should first discuss the concerns with the client rather than execute immediately.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client; discussions and resulting actions may encompass a variety of scenarios depending on how the requested unsuitable investment relates to client's full portfolio; in discussing the trade, member/candidate should focus on educating the investor on how the request deviates from current policy statement; member/candidate should require client to acknowledge the discussion, including that the trade is against the advice of the member/candidate because it is unsuitable for the portfolio.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Addressing Unsolicited Trading Requests: 'In cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate knows are unsuitable for a client, the member or candidate should refrain from making the trade until he or she discusses the concerns with the client... In discussing the trade, the member or candidate should focus on educating the investor on how the request deviates from the current policy statement. The member or candidate should require the client to acknowledge the discussion, including that the trade is against the advice of the member or candidate because it is unsuitable for the portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. After discussion, the trade may proceed with the client's acknowledgment (and possibly an updated IPS or unsupervised-trade documentation) — refusal-and-termination is not the prescribed course.

options.A: Index and mandate managers must still assess each end-investor's personal circumstances before trading.
options.B: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate.
options.C: Managing to a mandate exempts the manager from Standard III(C) entirely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For pooled mandates, suitability runs to consistency with the stated mandate; investor-level assessment belongs to those who recommend the fund.
rationales.B:
Full statement: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate; the responsibility of these members and candidates is to invest in a manner consistent with the stated mandate; member/candidate who serves as fund manager for large-cap income fund would not be following fund mandate by investing heavily in small-cap companies or start-ups whose stock is speculative in nature; members and candidates who manage pooled assets to a specific mandate are NOT responsible for determining suitability of the fund as an investment for investors who may be purchasing shares in the fund; responsibility for determining suitability of an investment for clients can be conferred only on members and candidates who have an advisory relationship with clients.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Managing to an Index or Mandate: 'Some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or an expected mandate. The responsibility of these members and candidates is to invest in a manner consistent with the stated mandate... Members and candidates who manage pooled assets to a specific mandate are not responsible for determining the suitability of the fund as an investment for investors who may be purchasing shares in the fund. The responsibility for determining the suitability of an investment for clients can be conferred only on members and candidates who have an advisory relationship with clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. III(C)(2) applies directly: recommendations and actions must be consistent with the stated objectives and constraints of the mandate.

options.A: There is no violation because Thompson's research and execution quality are average rather than deficient.
options.B: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients.
options.C: Directing brokerage in exchange for overhead reimbursement is acceptable if trade prices are competitive.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client brokerage was spent on services (rent, overhead) that do not benefit JNI's clients, and best execution was not obtained — both breach the duty.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients; because Jackson is not upholding his duty of loyalty, he is violating Standard III(A).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients. Because Jackson is not upholding his duty of loyalty, he is violating Standard III(A).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Client commissions are client assets; spending them on the manager's overhead violates loyalty regardless of price competitiveness.

options.A: The IPS need only be revisited when the client requests changes.
options.B: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client.
options.C: IPS review is required after executing material recommendations, to document what was done.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Review must occur at least annually and is the member's affirmative duty.
rationales.B:
Full statement: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client; effort to determine needs and circumstances of each client is NOT a one-time occurrence; investment recommendations or decisions are usually part of ongoing process that takes into account diversity and changing nature of portfolio and client characteristics; passage of time is bound to produce changes important with respect to investment objectives.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Updating an Investment Policy: 'An IPS should be reviewed at least annually and also prior to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client. The effort to determine the needs and circumstances of each client is not a one-time occurrence.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Review comes PRIOR to material recommendations or decisions, not afterward as documentation.

options.A: There is no violation because the performance figures are real and were genuinely earned by McCoy.
options.B: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind.
options.C: McCoy violated Standard V(A) by lacking a reasonable basis for the advertised strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Presenting G&P-era performance as Mastermind's without identifying its source is a material misrepresentation of the new firm's record.
rationales.B:
Full statement: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind; Standard III(D) does NOT prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at prior firm as part of performance track record AS LONG AS showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and person's specific role in achieving that performance; if McCoy chooses to use his past performance from G&P in Mastermind's advertising, he must make full disclosure of the source of the historical performance.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing an advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about the historical performance of Mastermind. Standard III(D) requires that members and candidates make reasonable efforts to ensure that performance information is a fair, accurate, and complete representation of an individual's or firm's performance. As a general matter, this standard does not prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at a prior firm as part of a performance track record as long as showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and the person's specific role in achieving that performance.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failing is performance presentation under III(D) — misattributed track record — not research diligence.

options.A: There is no violation because composite construction rules are left entirely to each firm's discretion.
options.B: Kilmer violated Standard III(D) by misrepresenting facts in promotional material sent to prospective clients, distorting his firm's performance record, and failing to include disclosures that would have clarified the presentation.
options.C: Kilmer's only obligation was to label the composite 'unaudited' in the promotional material.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Cherry-picked composition, undisclosed exclusions, and out-of-mandate inclusions distort the record — misrepresentation under III(D).
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Kilmer violated Standard III(D) by misrepresenting the facts in the promotional material sent to prospective clients, distorting his firm's performance record, and failing to include disclosures that would have clarified the presentation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. An 'unaudited' label does not cure a constructed, misleading performance record.

options.A: There is no violation because firms may select any attribution methodology they prefer.
options.B: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm.
options.C: Purell must restate all historical attribution under the original methodology before publishing new results.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Methodology choice is permitted, but CHANGES must be disclosed so clients can judge the performance presented.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm; changing methodology solely to make performance look better without proper disclosure is misleading; complete information is provided when clients have sufficient information to judge performance.

Correct. Per Example 6 (partial): 'Complete information is provided when clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm.' Selecting methodology to improve appearance without disclosure violates III(D).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Restatement is not the requirement; disclosure of the change and its effect is.

options.A: There is no violation because GIPS permits firms to amend composite-inclusion policies when circumstances warrant.
options.B: There is no violation because each company retains discretion to determine when new accounts enter its composites.
options.C: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Whatever flexibility exists to amend policies prospectively, Cupp applied an exception to capture a favorable result under the EXISTING stated policy.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Discretion is exercised through the firm's stated policy — adding the account early, against that policy, makes the monthly result inaccurate.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies; deviating from firm's stated policy to opportunistically capture favorable performance is a violation.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 9: Cupp deviated from the firm's stated policy of adding new accounts after the first full month specifically to include a favorable event. This produces an inaccurate calculation of monthly results per the firm's stated policies. Per Standard III(D), performance must be fair, accurate, and complete — including consistent application of stated policies.

options.A: There is no violation because Moody acted with charitable intent and expected no personal benefit.
options.B: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client.
options.C: There is no violation because the client's donation plans were not investment information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Good intentions do not excuse revealing confidential client information; III(E) was violated.
rationales.B:
Full statement: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client; well-intentioned motives do not excuse confidentiality breach.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Even though the attempt to help the Home for Indigent Widows was well intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Confidentiality covers information about the client learned in the professional relationship, financial plans included.

options.A: There is no violation because clients benefit when their manager maintains strong broker relationships.
options.B: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence.
options.C: Directed brokerage from Crayton was itself improper, since clients may not choose their broker.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Routing OTHER clients' trades to Scott without their knowledge — to win referrals — breaches loyalty and fair dealing; relationship-building is no defense.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence; may trade other accounts through Scott as reward for directing clients to Everett only if accounts receive best price and execution AND practice is disclosed to the accounts; because Everett does not disclose the directed trading, Everett violated Standard III(A).

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on the trades or receives a written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and that they are aware of the consequence for their accounts. Everett may trade other accounts through Scott as a reward for directing clients to Everett only if the accounts receive best price and execution and the practice is disclosed to the accounts. Because Everett does not disclose the directed trading, Everett violated Standard III(A).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. A client may direct brokerage; the violation is the undisclosed diversion of other clients' trades.

options.A: Samuel must refuse all cooperation, since Standard III(E) makes client confidentiality absolute.
options.B: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators.
options.C: Samuel should immediately deliver the pension records to the officials without any consultation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Confidentiality yields where illegal activity is suspected and law may require disclosure; the response is legal-counsel guidance, not blanket refusal.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators; Samuel may well have a duty to provide pension fund records her firm possesses to the government.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Samuel and her employer should seek the advice of legal counsel to determine the appropriate steps to take to protect the interests of the participants and beneficiaries of the pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators. Samuel may well have a duty to provide the pension fund records her firm possesses to the government.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The prescribed step is seeking legal counsel's advice to protect the plan's participants — not unadvised unilateral disclosure.

options.A: Bradford must immediately publicize his embezzlement suspicions to protect the market.
options.B: Bradford should check with his firm's compliance department or appropriate legal counsel to determine whether applicable securities regulations require reporting the CFO's financial records to authorities.
options.C: Bradford owes the CFO personal confidentiality and should take no action at all.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Going public first is not the course; checking with compliance or legal counsel about reporting obligations is.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Bradford should check with his firm's compliance department or appropriate legal counsel to determine whether applicable securities regulations require reporting the CFO's financial records to authorities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Suspected illegal activity is an exception to confidentiality; inaction is not an option.

options.A: Contacting the son violated Standard III(E), since Brennan never consented to disclosure of the trade request.
options.B: vulnerable investors — Standard III(A) requires diligently working to safeguard interests of all clients including potentially vulnerable investors.
options.C: Gonzales must execute the liquidation immediately because the client's instructions are controlling.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The son was established as SECONDARY CONTACT at onboarding — activating that arrangement protects a potentially vulnerable client rather than breaching confidentiality.
rationales.B:
Full statement: vulnerable investors — Standard III(A) requires diligently working to safeguard interests of all clients including potentially vulnerable investors; when member has established secondary contact at beginning of client relationship (trusted family member/legal adviser), that contact provides an avenue to prevent/address potential financial abuse; documentation of conversations and reasons for disclosing sensitive information should be retained in client files; Gonzales's proactive contact of designated secondary contact aligns with pre-established parameters and does not violate III(E).

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Guidance on Vulnerable Investors and Example 6: When a secondary contact was established at the beginning of the relationship (Brennan's son), Gonzales can properly reach out when concerns arise about ability to make informed decisions. Documentation of the conversations aligns with III(E) requirements.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Safeguarding a vulnerable investor under III(A) supports pausing and engaging the designated contact before executing a drastic, uncharacteristic order.

options.A: There is no violation because Rome's personal account pricing is separate from her professional duties.
options.B: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms.
options.C: Rome's only violation is failing to disclose her personal account to her employer.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Selecting Gray for TRUST business because of favorable personal pricing puts self-interest ahead of the accounts she owes loyalty to.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms; Rome is placing her own interests before those of her clients.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms. Rome is placing her own interests before those of her clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure would not cure it — the broker-selection decision itself breaches the duty of loyalty to the trust accounts.

options.A: As a hired expert, Hensley owes the law firm the same loyalty she would owe an advisory client.
options.B: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound.
options.C: Hensley should soften her testimony to avoid harming the party that engaged her.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Expert witnesses are bound by independence and objectivity — engagement does not create client-style loyalty over the testimony's content.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound; Hensley must NOT let law firm influence testimony she provides in the legal proceedings; in this situation, the law firm represents Hensley's employer and 'who is the client' is not well defined.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'In this situation, the law firm represents Hensley's employer and the aspect of "who is the client" is not well defined. When acting as an expert witness, Hensley is bound by the standard of independence and objectivity in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound. Hensley must not let the law firm influence the testimony she provides in the legal proceedings.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Shading testimony for the engaging party is precisely what the independence requirement forbids.

options.A: An annual summary statement satisfies the account-information practice for clients whose assets the member controls.
options.B: members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least QUARTERLY, an itemized statement showing funds and securities in custody or possession of member/candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period.
options.C: Account statements are the custodian's responsibility, so the member need send nothing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The practice is an itemized statement at least QUARTERLY, showing custody funds/securities and period activity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least QUARTERLY, an itemized statement showing funds and securities in custody or possession of member/candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period; (2) DISCLOSE to client where assets are maintained, as well as where or when they are moved; and (3) SEPARATE client's assets from any other party's assets, including member's/candidate's own assets.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Regular account information: Members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least quarterly, an itemized statement showing the funds and securities in the custody or possession of the member or candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period, (2) disclose to the client where the assets are maintained, as well as where or when they are moved, and (3) separate the client's assets from any other party's assets, including the member's or candidate's own assets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Members with control of client assets carry the reporting practice themselves.

options.A: Members with advance knowledge of a pending recommendation may trade for clients ahead of its release.
options.B: members and candidates who have prior knowledge of an investment recommendation should refrain from discussing or taking any action on the pending recommendation and must follow any firm policies regarding conduct prior to dissemination of investment recommendations.
options.C: Pre-release discussion of a pending recommendation is fine within the member's social circle.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Those with prior knowledge should refrain from ACTING on the pending recommendation until clients have had fair opportunity.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices: 'Publish guidelines for predissemination behavior: Members and candidates who have prior knowledge of an investment recommendation should refrain from discussing or taking any action on the pending recommendation and must follow any firm policies regarding conduct prior to dissemination of investment recommendations.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Discussing the pending recommendation is exactly what predissemination guidance restricts.

options.A: Recommendations should reach the largest fee-paying clients first, reflecting their economic importance.
options.B: members and candidates should establish procedures for timing of dissemination of investment recommendations so that all clients are treated fairly — that is, informed at approximately the same time.
options.C: Dissemination timing is immaterial so long as all clients eventually receive the recommendation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Fair treatment means no favored tier receives the recommendation in advance.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should establish procedures for timing of dissemination of investment recommendations so that all clients are treated fairly — that is, informed at approximately the same time; if firm is going to announce new recommendation, supervisory personnel should time the announcement to avoid placing any client or group of clients at unfair advantage relative to other clients; once distribution has occurred, member/candidate may follow up separately with individual clients, but must NOT give favored clients ADVANCE information when such advance notification may disadvantage other clients.

Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices: 'Simultaneous dissemination: Members and candidates should establish procedures for the timing of dissemination of investment recommendations so that all clients are treated fairly — that is, informed at approximately the same time. For example, if a firm is going to announce a new recommendation, supervisory personnel should time the announcement to avoid placing any client or group of clients at an unfair advantage relative to other clients. Once this distribution has occurred, the member or candidate may follow up separately with individual clients, but members and candidates must not give favored clients advance information when such advance notification may disadvantage other clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. TIMING is the point — procedures should get the information to all clients contemporaneously.

options.A: Allocation procedures should maximize aggregate firm commissions across client accounts.
options.B: when formulating procedures for allocating trades, should develop set of guiding principles that ensure: (1) FAIRNESS to advisory clients, both in priority of execution of orders and in allocation of price obtained in execution of block orders or trades.
options.C: Guiding principles for allocation address execution priority only, not the accuracy of trade records.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The principles center on FAIRNESS to advisory clients in execution priority and allocation, plus timeliness and accuracy.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when formulating procedures for allocating trades, should develop set of guiding principles that ensure: (1) FAIRNESS to advisory clients, both in priority of execution of orders and in allocation of price obtained in execution of block orders or trades; (2) TIMELINESS AND EFFICIENCY in execution of orders; and (3) ACCURACY of member's or candidate's records as to trade orders and client account positions.

Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices — Develop and document trade allocation procedures: 'When formulating procedures for allocating trades, members and candidates should develop a set of guiding principles that ensure fairness to advisory clients, both in priority of execution of orders and in the allocation of the price obtained in execution of block orders or trades, timeliness and efficiency in the execution of orders, and accuracy of the member's or candidate's records as to trade orders and client account positions.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Timely, efficient order execution AND accuracy of trade records/client positions are within the principles.

options.A: Maintaining a master list of clients and their holdings is discouraged as a confidentiality hazard.
options.B: members and candidates should maintain a list of all clients and the securities or other investments each client holds in order to facilitate notification of customers or clients of a change in an investment recommendation.
options.C: A holdings list is needed only for discretionary accounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The list is a recommended tool enabling notification of all affected customers when action is warranted.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should maintain a list of all clients and the securities or other investments each client holds in order to facilitate notification of customers or clients of a change in an investment recommendation; if a particular security or other investment is to be sold, such a list can be used to ensure that all holders are treated fairly in the liquidation of that particular investment.

Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices: 'Maintain a list of clients and their holdings: Members and candidates should maintain a list of all clients and the securities or other investments each client holds in order to facilitate notification of customers or clients of a change in an investment recommendation. If a particular security or other investment is to be sold, such a list can be used to ensure that all holders are treated fairly in the liquidation of that particular investment.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fair-dealing notification duties extend to clients generally, so the list covers all clients and their holdings.

options.A: A suitability test begins and ends with the investment's expected return relative to its peers.
options.B: appropriate suitability test procedures require investment professional to look BEYOND return potential of the investment and include: analysis of the impact on the portfolio's diversification.
options.C: Suitability procedures assess the security's risk in isolation from the client's existing portfolio.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The test looks BEYOND return potential — to portfolio impact, risk-profile fit, and strategy consistency.
rationales.B:
Full statement: appropriate suitability test procedures require investment professional to look BEYOND return potential of the investment and include: analysis of the impact on the portfolio's diversification; a comparison of investment risks with client's assessed risk tolerance; and the fit of the investment with the required investment strategy.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Compliance Practices: 'Suitability Test Policies: Members and candidates must comply with their firm's policies and procedures relating to suitability. Appropriate suitability test procedures require the investment professional to look beyond the return potential of the investment and include the following: an analysis of the impact on the portfolio's diversification, a comparison of the investment risks with the client's assessed risk tolerance, and the fit of the investment with the required investment strategy.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Impact on the diversification and composition of the existing portfolio is a core element.

options.A: Once executed, the IPS remains fixed so that performance can be judged against a stable baseline.
options.B: members and candidates should periodically review the investor's objectives and constraints and reflect any changes in the client's circumstances in an updated IPS.
options.C: IPS updates are triggered only by market events, not by changes in the client's own circumstances.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Objectives and constraints must be reviewed periodically and the IPS UPDATED for changed circumstances.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should periodically review the investor's objectives and constraints and reflect any changes in the client's circumstances in an updated IPS; should regularly compare client constraints with capital market expectations to arrive at appropriate asset allocation; changes in either factor may result in fundamental change in asset allocation; should review each client's IPS annually unless business or other reasons, such as major change in market conditions, dictate more frequent review.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Compliance Practices — Regular Updates: 'Members and candidates should periodically review the investor's objectives and constraints and reflect any changes in the client's circumstances in an updated IPS. Members and candidates should regularly compare client constraints with capital market expectations to arrive at an appropriate asset allocation. Changes in either factor may result in a fundamental change in asset allocation. Members and candidates should review each client's IPS annually unless business or other reasons, such as a major change in market conditions, dictate more frequent review.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Client circumstance changes are the primary update trigger.

options.A: When uncertain about the right course, the member should proceed unilaterally on professional judgment alone.
options.B: if member/candidate is uncertain about appropriate course of action with respect to client, member/candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if they were the client.
options.C: Uncertainty should be resolved by following whatever maximizes the account's short-term return.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The guidance is to consider what one would expect or demand as the client — and, in practice, to disclose and seek approval.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if member/candidate is uncertain about appropriate course of action with respect to client, member/candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if they were the client; if in doubt, member/candidate should disclose the questionable matter in WRITING to the client and OBTAIN CLIENT APPROVAL.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Client approval: If a member or candidate is uncertain about the appropriate course of action with respect to a client, the member or candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if the member or candidate were the client. If in doubt, a member or candidate should disclose the questionable matter in writing to the client and obtain client approval.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The touchstone is the client's perspective and interests, not short-term return.

options.A: Members should confine their inquiry to the assets they manage and avoid asking about outside holdings.
options.B: members and candidates should encourage their clients to fully disclose their complete financial portfolio, including those portions not managed by the member or candidate, to facilitate an effective suitability determination.
options.C: Clients who decline to reveal outside assets cannot be advised at all.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should ENCOURAGE disclosure of the complete financial portfolio, including unmanaged portions, for sound suitability analysis.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should encourage their clients to fully disclose their complete financial portfolio, including those portions not managed by the member or candidate, to facilitate an effective suitability determination; if clients withhold information about their financial portfolios, the suitability analysis conducted by members and candidates must be based on the information provided and cannot be expected to be complete.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Updating an Investment Policy: 'Members and candidates should encourage their clients to fully disclose their complete financial portfolio, including those portions not managed by the member or candidate, to facilitate an effective suitability determination. If clients withhold information about their financial portfolios, the suitability analysis conducted by members and candidates must be based on the information provided and cannot be expected to be complete.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Advice may proceed on available information; the practice is encouragement, not a precondition.

options.A: GIPS compliance is discouraged for smaller managers because of its cost and complexity.
options.B: for members and candidates who are showing performance history of assets they manage, compliance with GIPS standards is the BEST METHOD to meet their obligations under Standard III(D).
options.C: GIPS compliance is legally mandated for any member presenting a performance history.

rationales.A: Incorrect. GIPS compliance is recommended as the BEST method of meeting III(D) obligations, without a size carve-out.
rationales.B:
Full statement: for members and candidates who are showing performance history of assets they manage, compliance with GIPS standards is the BEST METHOD to meet their obligations under Standard III(D); members and candidates should encourage their firms to comply with GIPS® standards.

Correct. Per Standard III(D) Compliance Practices — Compliance with the GIPS Standards: 'For members and candidates who are showing the performance history of the assets they manage, compliance with the GIPS standards is the best method to meet their obligations under Standard III(D). Members and candidates should encourage their firms to comply with the GIPS® standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. GIPS is voluntary; it is the best method, not a legal mandate, for satisfying the standard.

options.A: Non-GIPS presenters satisfy III(D) by using identical boilerplate for every audience.
options.B: considering knowledge and sophistication of audience to whom performance presentation is addressed when determining what information to provide and tailoring it accordingly.
options.C: Audience sophistication may not be considered, since all clients must receive identical detail.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The guidance is to consider the audience's knowledge and sophistication and TAILOR the information accordingly.
rationales.B:
Full statement: considering knowledge and sophistication of audience to whom performance presentation is addressed when determining what information to provide and tailoring it accordingly; presenting performance of COMPOSITE of similar portfolios rather than using single representative account; INCLUDING TERMINATED ACCOUNTS as part of any composite performance history; including DISCLOSURES that fully explain performance results being reported (stating when appropriate that results are simulated when model results are used; clearly indicating when performance record is that of prior entity; disclosing whether performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax); MAINTAINING data and records used to calculate performance being presented.

Correct. Per Standard III(D) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates whose firms do not comply with the GIPS standards can meet their obligations under Standard III(D) to present fair, accurate, and complete investment performance history by, among other things, considering the knowledge and sophistication of the audience to whom a performance presentation is addressed when determining what information to provide and tailoring it accordingly, presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account, including terminated accounts as part of any composite performance history, including disclosures that fully explain the performance results being reported (for example, stating, when appropriate, that results are simulated when model results are used; clearly indicating when the performance record is that of a prior entity; or disclosing whether the performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax), and maintaining the data and records used to calculate the performance being presented.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Tailoring to the audience is expressly recommended — fairness does not mean uniformity of detail.

options.A: A brief presentation carries no further obligations once distributed.
options.B: if presentation is brief, member/candidate must make available to clients and prospects, ON REQUEST, the detailed information supporting that communication.
options.C: Supporting detail must be proactively mailed to every recipient of a brief presentation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Detailed supporting information must be made AVAILABLE ON REQUEST.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if presentation is brief, member/candidate must make available to clients and prospects, ON REQUEST, the detailed information supporting that communication; best practice dictates that brief presentations include a reference to the LIMITED NATURE of the information provided.

Correct. Per Standard III(D) Guidance: 'If the presentation is brief, the member or candidate must make available to clients and prospects, on request, the detailed information supporting that communication. Best practice dictates that brief presentations include a reference to the limited nature of the information provided.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The obligation is availability on request, not unsolicited distribution.

options.A: Standard III(D) categorically prohibits presenting any performance earned while employed at a prior firm, because that record belongs to the former employer and cannot form part of a new track record.
options.B: Standard III(D) does NOT prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at a prior firm as part of a performance track record AS LONG AS showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about WHERE the performance took place and the person's SPECIFIC ROLE in achieving that performance.
options.C: Performance earned at a prior firm may be presented without qualification or disclosure once the member has changed employers, since the results were genuinely produced by the same individual.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Prior-firm history is permitted when accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where and how it was earned.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard III(D) — Example 3 Comment (McCoy): 'Standard III(D) does not prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at a prior firm as part of a performance track record as long as showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and the person's specific role in achieving that performance.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Unqualified presentation misattributes the record; disclosure of its origin is the condition.

options.A: Client information may be shared with any colleague at the firm, since all employees owe the same duties.
options.B: members and candidates should avoid disclosing any information received from a client EXCEPT to authorized fellow employees who are also working for the client.
options.C: Client information may never be shared with anyone, including team members serving the client.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Sharing is limited to AUTHORIZED fellow employees who are also working for that client.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should avoid disclosing any information received from a client EXCEPT to authorized fellow employees who are also working for the client; must understand and follow their firm's electronic information communication and storage procedures; if firm does not have procedures in place, members/candidates should encourage development of procedures that appropriately reflect firm's size and business operations; should encourage their firm to conduct regular periodic training on confidentiality procedures for all firm personnel, including noninvestment staff who have routine direct contact with clients and their records.

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should avoid disclosing any information received from a client except to authorized fellow employees who are also working for the client. Members and candidates must understand and follow their firm's electronic information communication and storage procedures. If the firm does not have procedures in place, members and candidates should encourage the development of procedures that appropriately reflect the firm's size and business operations. Members and candidates should encourage their firm to conduct regular periodic training on confidentiality procedures for all firm personnel, including noninvestment staff who have routine direct contact with clients and their records.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Authorized colleagues on the client's service team are the recognized exception.

options.A: A secondary contact should be sought only after signs of diminished capacity appear.
options.B: best practice for members and candidates is to establish a SECONDARY CONTACT at the beginning of the client relationship.
options.C: Establishing secondary contacts is discouraged as an intrusion into client privacy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Best practice is establishing the secondary contact at the BEGINNING of the relationship, before any need arises.
rationales.B:
Full statement: best practice for members and candidates is to establish a SECONDARY CONTACT at the beginning of the client relationship; contact could be trusted family member, legal adviser, or some other third-party intermediary whom clients permit contacting should concerns arise about their ability to make informed decisions about their finances; nominated secondary contact provides an avenue to prevent and/or address potential financial abuse of the client.

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Guidance on Vulnerable Investors: 'Best practice for members and candidates is to establish a secondary contact at the beginning of the client relationship. This contact could be a trusted family member, a legal adviser, or some other third-party intermediary whom clients permit contacting should concerns arise about their ability to make informed decisions about their finances. The nominated secondary contact provides members and candidates an avenue to prevent and/or address potential financial abuse of the client.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. A client-designated contact is a protective best practice, not a privacy intrusion.

options.A: Vulnerable-investor protection is exclusively a legal-guardian matter outside the member's role.
options.B: steps to protect interests of vulnerable investors: complying with any firm policies and procedures specifically dealing with vulnerable clients.
options.C: Firm policies on vulnerable clients are optional guidance a member may set aside case by case.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members take affirmative protective steps, starting with compliance with firm policies for vulnerable clients.
rationales.B:
Full statement: steps to protect interests of vulnerable investors: complying with any firm policies and procedures specifically dealing with vulnerable clients; asking for secondary contact during establishment of every account; undertaking training and education to understand issues related to vulnerable investors; undergoing training on how to interact and address issues with clients who may exhibit diminished mental capacity; following internal firm reporting procedures when concerns are raised; and implementing additional compliance review for accounts of vulnerable investors.

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should take steps to protect the interests of vulnerable investors by complying with any firm policies and procedures specifically dealing with vulnerable clients, asking for a secondary contact during the establishment of every account, undertaking training and education to understand issues related to vulnerable investors, undergoing training on how to interact and address issues with clients who may exhibit diminished mental capacity, following internal firm reporting procedures when concerns are raised, and implementing additional compliance review for the accounts of vulnerable investors.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Complying with the firm's vulnerable-client policies and procedures is the listed step, not optional guidance.

options.A: Members must become information-technology experts to discharge their confidentiality duties.
options.B: because of ever-increasing use of electronically stored information, members and candidates need to be particularly aware of potential accidental disclosures.
options.C: Electronic storage eliminates disclosure risk, so no special awareness is needed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The expectation is heightened AWARENESS of accidental-disclosure risk and adherence to firm policies — not IT expertise.
rationales.B:
Full statement: because of ever-increasing use of electronically stored information, members and candidates need to be particularly aware of potential accidental disclosures; many employers have strict policies about how to electronically communicate sensitive client information and store client information on personal laptops, mobile devices, or external storage devices or systems; Standard III(E) does NOT require members or candidates to become experts in information security technology, but they should have a thorough understanding of policies of their employer for ensuring the security of confidential information maintained by the firm.

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Guidance on Electronic Information and Security: 'Because of the ever-increasing use of electronically stored information, members and candidates need to be particularly aware of potential accidental disclosures. Many employers have strict policies about how to electronically communicate sensitive client information and store client information on personal laptops, mobile devices, or external storage devices or systems. Standard III(E) does not require members or candidates to become experts in information security technology, but they should have a thorough understanding of the policies of their employer for ensuring the security of confidential information maintained by the firm.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Electronic storage INCREASES accidental-disclosure exposure; that is the reason for the guidance.

options.A: Diversification is optional under III(A) whenever the manager holds high conviction in a position.
options.B: members and candidates should DIVERSIFY investments to reduce the risk of loss, UNLESS diversification is not consistent with plan guidelines or is contrary to the account objectives.
options.C: Managers must diversify even when plan guidelines or account terms direct concentration.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Diversification to reduce loss risk is the default duty; conviction does not suspend it.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Diversify: Members and candidates should diversify investments to reduce the risk of loss, unless diversification is not consistent with plan guidelines or is contrary to the account objectives.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The duty yields where diversification is inconsistent with plan guidelines or contrary to the account's terms.

options.A: Standard III(E) requires members to refuse CFA Institute Professional Conduct requests involving client matters.
options.B: Standard III(E) does NOT prevent members and candidates from cooperating with an investigation by Professional Conduct (PC) at CFA Institute.
options.C: Cooperation with a PC investigation requires prior written consent from every affected client.

rationales.A: Incorrect. III(E) does NOT prevent cooperation with a Professional Conduct investigation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard III(E) does NOT prevent members and candidates from cooperating with an investigation by Professional Conduct (PC) at CFA Institute; instead, members and candidates MUST cooperate with investigations into their conduct unless prevented from doing so by law; under CFA Institute Rules of Procedure for Conduct Related to the Profession (as amended and restated 1 January 2022), members and candidates are also required to cooperate with investigations into the conduct of OTHERS; PC will exercise reasonable care to ensure that all documents and information it receives during an investigation remain confidential.

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Guidance on Professional Conduct Investigations by CFA Institute: 'Standard III(E) does not prevent members and candidates from cooperating with an investigation by Professional Conduct (PC) at CFA Institute. Instead, members and candidates must cooperate with investigations into their conduct unless prevented from doing so by law. Under the CFA Institute Rules of Procedure for Conduct Related to the Profession (as amended and restated 1 January 2022), members and candidates are also required to cooperate with investigations into the conduct of others. PC will exercise reasonable care to ensure that all documents and information it receives during an investigation remain confidential.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No client-consent precondition attaches to PC cooperation under the standard.

options.A: Directed brokerage requires no disclosure because the client, not the manager, chose the broker.
options.B: when clients direct manager to use specific brokerage firm for trades (directed brokerage), because brokerage commission is an asset of the client and is used to benefit that client, not the manager, this practice does not violate duty of loyalty.
options.C: Members should refuse all directed-brokerage arrangements as inherent best-execution violations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Because commissions are the client's asset, the member should disclose that the direction may cost the client best execution.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when clients direct manager to use specific brokerage firm for trades (directed brokerage), because brokerage commission is an asset of the client and is used to benefit that client, not the manager, this practice does not violate duty of loyalty; the member or candidate should, however, seek CONFIRMATION from the client that goods or services purchased from the brokerage will benefit the account beneficiaries; in addition, member or candidate should DISCLOSE to the client that the client may not be getting best execution from the directed brokerage.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Guidance on Soft Commission Policies: 'from time to time, some clients will direct a manager to use a specific brokerage firm for all or some of the client's trades... The member or candidate should, however, seek confirmation from the client that the goods or services purchased from the brokerage will benefit the account beneficiaries. In addition, the member or candidate should disclose to the client that the client may not be getting best execution from the directed brokerage.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Directed brokerage is permissible; the duty is disclosure of the potential best-execution consequence.

options.A: The whole trading floor should be alerted to a pending recommendation so execution can be staged efficiently.
options.B: members and candidates should make reasonable efforts to LIMIT the number of people who are aware that a recommendation is going to be disseminated.
options.C: Awareness of a pending recommendation may extend to anyone who signs a confidentiality notice.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The practice is to LIMIT the number of people aware that a recommendation is coming.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices: 'Limit the number of people involved: Members and candidates should make reasonable efforts to limit the number of people who are aware that a recommendation is going to be disseminated.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Signed notices do not substitute for minimizing awareness; the smaller the circle, the lower the front-running risk.
